question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any public health information programmes promoted by the Public Health Agency, in each of the last five years, including (i) the reason for undertaking the campaign; and (ii) a breakdown of any budget spent, including on the media used.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, since the publication of the High Street Taskforce Report in March 2022, to detail any action (i) taken by their office to deliver a 21st Century High Street; (ii) taken to deliver on the report, including areas of success and areas of failure; (iii) they plan to take to deliver on the report, given the current economic environment in our high streets.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that those in receipt of £100 home heating oil grants will get value for money when purchasing oil.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he has had with home heating oil suppliers and distributors to ensure there will be no price gouging, and that small loads can be delivered without price penalty; and (ii) whether he will promote the use of oil buying clubs.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) action he is taking to protect the closed FLAME Gasworks Museum in Carrickfergus, including ensuring that the building, equipment and artefacts are preserved; and (ii) plans his Department has to secure the future of the museum and reopen it to the public.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why the recent road resurfacing work, at the junction of Old Carrick Road and Monkstown Road, Newtownabbey, failed to include a significant subsidence dip in the Monkstown Road, less than one meter from the end of the resurfacing work; and (ii) any action her Department will take to address this road issue.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the availability of scalp cooling for patients in receipt of certain types of chemotherapy, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Scalp cooling treatments are not currently commissioned within Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland, and are not part of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommended care pathway for managing hair loss during chemotherapy.  The routine commissioning of this treatment can only be considered based on the long-term evidence of efficacy, available funding and the relative priority of this service in the context of other service development proposals and the overall financial position.My Department’s position can be reviewed if new or updated NICE recommendations emerge that support scalp cooling for cancer patients. I remain committed to aligning our commissioning approach with the strongest available evidence and national guidance as it develops.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) how the professional photographs taken during the St Patrick’s Day programme in Washington D.C. will be distributed and used; and (ii) any assurances that they will not be made available for personal or party political purposes.
answer_text: Photography is an essential part of government and ministerial communications. Photographs taken during a series of events to mark St Patrick’s Week in Washington D.C. were distributed via the department’s social media channels and to media via press releases. Once published, they are in the public domain. As with all ministerial engagements, photographs taken during the series of events in Washington D.C. are held by the department and may be used for future communication purposes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any public benefit derived from expenditure of almost £5,000 on professional photography during the St Patrick’s Day programme in Washington D.C.
answer_text: We were able to secure professional, press-quality images capturing the wide range of events attended by the deputy First Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work of the Housing for All Ministerial Panel - Housing Thematic Sub-Group.
answer_text: The “Housing for All” Ministerial Panel Housing Thematic Sub-group is chaired by NIHE and acts as an advisory body to help guide the ‘Housing for All’ Shared Housing Programme. The sub-group shares experience in delivery of shared communities and progress on good relations outcomes. The latest meeting took place on Wednesday, 4 March 2026, and included:Discussion of the Housing for All Shared Housing Programme for 2025/26, which incorporated six new developments across Northern Ireland.the importance of connectivity between new and existing shared housing schemes, as well as the opportunities presented by developments in new locations, including Maghera and Craigavon.Business planning for 2026/27, including further development of shared housing and shared spaces in citycentre locations, and the continued evolution of Good Relations Plans. Members received presentations on T:BUC and the Peace Builders Project, a planned good relations programme in South Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that the feasibility review for the Carrickfergus Academy new build concluded in November 2025, to outline when the business case is expected to be complete.
answer_text: It is anticipated that drafting of the Business Case will be completed by the end of March 2026.The Business Case will then be reviewed by Departmental economists before being issued to the Department of Finance for consideration. It is anticipated that a decision on the Business Case will be issued in Summer 2026.

To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Speeding up Justice programme.

answer_text: Pursuant to the answer provided for AQW 36484/22-27, organisations that have been involved in projects to address issues around racial and social cohesion in East Antrim are Larne YMCA and the Inter-Ethnic Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will publish further details on the funding and implementation of hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose in type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 - Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable to Northern Ireland. Subsequently, I supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop a five-year implementation plan, outlining an appropriate timescale for rollout. Similar to other nations, service delivery will be phased over a 5-year period in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation.Work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support. The Regional Insulin Pump service, led by Belfast Trust, in partnership with remaining Trusts, will deliver this plan, as a service development.  £1.839m of funding annually has been secured to support implementation of this plan, with the final stages of commissioning to be completed in the coming weeks.Funding is required for devices, in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  The implementation plan is in the final stages of drafting and will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.         In line with NICE guidance, this includes a process of prioritisation and recommended eligibility criteria. However, the most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the Trust and their clinicians to determine, in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer. Once finalised, information will be communicated to all stakeholders in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the relocation of Islandmagee Primary School on the Low Road, whether the part-time 20mph speed limit will be applied at the new school location.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.The introduction of part-time 20mph zones at schools across the north is a significant intervention that has helped to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis and in recognition of this intervention I announced a third tranche of 40 schools bringing the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This tranche focused on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies however I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas that would benefit from a reduction in the speed limit, and I am currently undertaking a consultation on Speed Limits which includes the introduction of permanent 20mph zones.Islandmagee Primary School previously benefitted from a 20mph zone due to its location on a road where the national speed limit applied, however its relocation within a 30mph speed limit means that it does not meet the current criteria for inclusion in this third tranche.Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any urgent actions he is taking to address delays in GP-delivered childhood vaccinations; and (ii) the number of children whose routine vaccinations are overdue.
answer_text: Childhood vaccinations are provided by GPs to children registered with their practice.  There have been several recent changes in the childhood vaccination schedule, and information has been provided to inform GPs and their practice teams about the new schedule, including through on-line training, and via workshops. Childhood vaccinations are currently recorded on the Child Health System (CHS), which is also used to schedule vaccination appointments. The Public Health Agency, CHS, and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group have all engaged with GP practices to help improve understanding of the system. In addition, work to replace CHS through Encompass is ongoing. This will streamline and digitalise processes relating to how children are called and scheduled for routine immunisations. There can be many reasons for children being overdue for vaccination, including varying numbers of children becoming eligible for each vaccine each week, practice capacity to match that demand, and children not being brought for vaccination appointments.Practices are regularly informed by CHS about children who are overdue, and practice level teams can look at those variable reasons to consider either how their vaccinations are scheduled or whether to engage with parents.    My Department does not hold a list of children who are overdue for vaccination. I would encourage any parent who is concerned that their child maybe overdue a vaccination to contact their GP practice to make the appropriate arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any assessment that has been made of the recent storm damage to the sea walls along the Carrickfergus coastline at Rhanbuoy Park.
answer_text: My officials initially assessed the damage to the sea wall, at Rhanbuoy Park, immediately following Storm Bram, which occurred on Tuesday 9 December 2025.Temporary measures were put in place to ensure public safety and mitigate against future flooding, until permanent repairs can be completed.As you will appreciate work of this nature is susceptible to the elements; therefore, repairs will be scheduled during more amenable weather conditions.Officials continue to carry out ad-hoc inspections at this location, especially following adverse weather events and can advise that no reported incidents were received as a result of Storm Chandra.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the responsibility for the maintenance of the footpath on the Islandmagee Road, Whitehead, opposite Whitehead Primary School, rests with her Department or Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.
answer_text: I can confirm that the responsibility for maintenance of the footpath at the location specified rests with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how his Department ensures a consistent approach to social value in the public sector.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department, or any of its agencies, will be represented either in person or online at the European Cancer Screening and Early Detection Policy Summit for Newly Implementable Programmes for Prostate, Lung, and Gastric Cancer Screening, in Brussels on 17 and 18 February 2026.
answer_text: My Department or the Public Health Agency have not been invited to attend this event.Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent committee which advises the four UK health Departments on screening matters.  My Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how dialysis patients, who wish to travel to the Republic of Ireland, arrange dialysis treatment.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have a dialysis co-ordinator who assists patients to make arrangement for dialysis if they travel to the Republic of Ireland.  They often direct patients and their families to Irish Kidney Association or Freedom, dialysis holiday specialists who can help identify suitable units close to holiday venue and assist in arranging holiday dialysis. The dialysis coordinator can assist patients in making arrangements with that particular centre once they have their dates and destinations planned. They will normally reach out to the team in the destination centre to discuss the patient, what their medical needs are and share their current dialysis therapy plan to ensure the centre can accommodate the safe delivery of dialysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how dialysis patients avail of reimbursement costs of dialysis on a cruise with an approved supplier, similar to patients who receive reimbursement in Scotland, England and Wales.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, further to media reports, to outline why their Department does not hold information in relation to whether or not the deputy First Minister recused herself from discussions on finances relating to the Education Authority.
answer_text: Ministers are not required to recuse themselves from discussion, and as such there is no recorded information in relation to recusals. Ministers are required to declare an interest where they are proposing or supporting an action which is likely to, or perceived to, have a direct benefit to themselves or to another person or organisation with whom they have a close personal relationship or interest.    Such an interest was declared by the deputy First Minister in relation to these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the reported shortages of salt and gritting resources in Mid and East Antrim Borough Council, due to supply issues with her Department, including (i) the nature and duration of the supply issues; (ii) any contingency arrangements that were in place; and (iii) any steps being taken to prevent a recurrence.
answer_text: The Department provides gritting salt under existing agreements with a number of councils, for the removal of snow and ice from town centre footways and pedestrian areas. These agreements are activated during prolonged periods of severe weather.Priority is given to the provision of salt for the treatment of the gritted road network, after which the Department makes salt available to local Councils for the treatment of town centre footways and pedestrian areas. There are currently no issues impacting this supply of salt.This agreement is for salt supplies in addition to stocks held by Councils for the treatment of council facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the rock face cladding at the sea defences wall on the Belfast Road dual carriageway, Carrickfergus, will be replaced.
answer_text: My officials have confirmed that the cladding to the sea wall along the Belfast Road, Carrickfergus is not a structural issue and at present, due to other high priority work, my Department has no plans to replace it. While there are many areas that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, maintenance work is taken forward through the prioritisation of that deemed most in need for intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the investigation, by the Office for Environmental Protection, into her Department and its role in the regulation of sewage discharges into Belfast Lough.
answer_text: I welcome the Office for Environmental Protection’s investigation into the regulation of sewage discharges into Belfast Lough and can advise that my Department will cooperate fully with this investigation. However, it is important to note that the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs’ Northern Ireland Environment Agency regulates the sewage discharges into Belfast Lough, not DFI.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, given that MLA remuneration is currently under independent review, whether it has any plans to review MLAs' staff pay scales and grading structures.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the December Monitoring Round.
answer_text: The December Monitoring round was commissioned on 22 October, with departmental returns due last Thursday 20 November. These returns, combined with the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget announcement on 26 November, will inform the level of funding available for allocation in the December Monitoring round. I will bring proposals to the Executive for agreement shortly after the Autumn Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to address health inequalities arising from the absence of a lung cancer screening programme, particularly in deprived areas with a higher smoking prevalence.
answer_text: Reducing health inequalities is one of my key areas of focus and shortening the period of life spent in ill-health will help to reduce pressures on our health services and improve the quality of life for many of our populationSmoking is one of the key drivers of health inequalities in NI – with the smoking death rate in the most deprived communities twice as high as in the least deprived communities. Similarly, the NI Cancer Registry published statistics in July 2025 on lung cancer incidence shows that lung cancer incidence rates in the most deprived areas are almost double the rate seen in the least deprived areas. The implementation of the Tobacco and Vapes Bill will therefore be a key action in helping to reduce these health inequalities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rational for Northern Ireland remaining the only UK region without a lung cancer screening programme or pilot, despite evidence from the UK National Screening Committee and the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry that many cases are diagnosed through emergency presentations which are associated with poorer outcomes.
answer_text: As stated in the three-year Health and Social Care Plan, it is intended that by April 2027, subject to additional funding, to have a targeted lung screening programme in Northern Ireland. We are at the early stages of scoping out what the requirements of the new programme for Northern Ireland will be. This requires a significant amount of work on the supporting pathways (including call/recall, additional diagnostics, workforce, pathways for incidental findings and treatment services) to be undertaken and with consideration to the symptomatic pathways.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions they have taken to resolve the impasse over the Maze Long Kesh site, to allow for its future development, since they have taken up office.
answer_text: We have met with the Board of the Maze/Long Kesh Development Corporation (MLKDC). Members of the Board have also met on several occasions with the TEO Permanent Secretary. We have received advice from officials on the immediate priorities for the site, and this is under consideration. We recognise the potential of the site and the need to move forward with consensus. No decisions have been made yet on the future development of the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timeline for when a decision will be made on the introduction of low dose computed tomography lung cancer screening.
answer_text: As stated in the three-year Health and Social Care Plan, it is intended that by April 2027, subject to additional funding, to have a targeted lung screening programme in Northern Ireland.The aim of this new targeted screening programme is to improve earlier diagnosis. If the individual has a higher risk of developing cancer, they will be offered a low dose Computerised Tomography (CT) of their lungs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health given that the April 2025 Northern Ireland Cancer Registry report Routes to Diagnosis highlights a disproportionately high rate of emergency presentations for lung cancer, to detail any steps his Department is taking to reduce late-stage diagnosis and improve survival rates.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any action he is taking to ensure patients in Northern Ireland are afforded the same access to clinical trials as patients in other parts of the UK.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment made of the impact of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an assessment of the impact of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.	 	Following the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 employing authorities (Education Authority (EA) and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS)) and Board of Governors were required to review their recruitment and selection procedures, ensuring that they address the implications of fair employment legislation.Through the Teachers’ Negotiation Committee (TNC) a Fair Employment Working Group was established to consider the implications of the Act to ensure a co-ordinated implementation of the legislation.Through the working group the monitoring requirements and form to be used were agreed with the employing authorities (EA and CCMS). They are each required to report to the Equality Commission Northern Ireland (ECNI) in an agreed return. All other schools report independently to the ECNI.My Department has ensured that the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has been implemented across all grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of students who have taken advantage of (i) the Turing Scheme; and (ii) Erasmus, via the Irish Government Scheme for Northern Ireland, since the United Kingdom left the European Union.
answer_text: Further Education Colleges have provided data which indicates that since 31 January 2020, 796 FE students have secured a place on the Turing Scheme and 203 students have participated in the Irish Government Mobility Scheme. It should be noted that the colleges have stated that some figures are unavailable for the year 2021/22 and so the actual number could be higher. Higher Education Institutions have provided data to my Department which indicates 1,185 higher education students have secured a placement under the Turing Scheme and 1,773 higher education students have participated in the Irish Government Mobility Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps he is taking to ensure that people can access cancer trials despite hold-ups for signing up by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care (HSC) Research & Development (R&D) Division within the Public Health Agency are responsible for the administration of the Department’s budgetary allocation for Research & Development, including support for research offices in each of the acute Trusts.At present, HSC R&D do not specifically commission cancer research studies. Rather, they provide funding to cancer research infrastructure such as the Northern Ireland Cancer Trials Network (NICTN), the Belfast Experimental Medicine Centre and the Northern Ireland Biobank. Responsibility for the approval of research studies in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust lies with the Trust Research Office.  The Trust has agreed to implement prioritised actions to meet the UK wide 150-day target for clinical trial study set-up, and funding has been provided by the HSC R&D Division in support of this.   I acknowledge the importance of cancer clinical trials and research and recognise that they have historically been underfunded in Northern Ireland.  It is clear that significant and sustained investment in cancer clinical trials and research is urgently required. However, in the context of ongoing budgetary challenges being faced by the Department, this is not possible at this point in time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps he is taking to prioritise brain cancer within the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 aims to transform cancer services by ensuring equitable and timely access to the most effective, evidence-based referral, diagnosis, treatment and support for people impacted by a cancer diagnosis across our communities, rather than prioritising specific cancers.It is important that we remain committed to the transformation of cancer services through the implementation of the Cancer Strategy, this will help to ensure equitable access across all cancer services, including those provided for brain cancer patients.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any assessment made of the impact of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) whether any monitoring or evaluation of its implementation has been undertaken, or is planned.
answer_text: The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act 2022 (Teachers Act) came into effect on 12th May 2024. Responsibility for fair employment monitoring, including that of teachers, rests with the Equality Commission for NI (ECNI).To date, no assessment has been made of the impact of the Teachers Act.  TEO officials will remain engaged with ECNI and the Department of Education as necessary regarding the smooth implementation of the Teachers Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the next steps to develop Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan.
answer_text: The consultation on Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan recently closed and I want to express my gratitude to the 224 individuals, businesses and organisations who provided consultation responses and to the approximately 700 people who attended the wide range of consultation events across Northern Ireland. It is important that we take the appropriate time to consider these valuable insights and that the views of consultees are incorporated into the final Plan as appropriate. DAERA will be working with other Departments to update the document and finalise the Climate Action Plan for Executive consideration as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the UK-wide BEST4 trial of capsule sponge testing for Barrett’s oesophagus and oesophageal cancer has already been approved in Great Britain and is already recruiting, to detail what immediate action will his Department take to ensure patients can access this UK-wide clinical trial, noting that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has had the fully funded NI trial documentation for over a year for approval.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is currently unable to participate in the BEST4 trial due to aspects of the trial not being compliant with the European Union In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation (EU IVDR 2017/746). The sponsor of the trial, Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, will be required to submit an application to the regulator MHRA, to enable the trial to progress.The Local Information Pack (LIP) application for the BEST 4 study was received by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Research Office from the study sponsor on 4 October 2024.  The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are still awaiting the release of the Study Wide Governance Report from the PHA HSC Approvals Service, to facilitate their participation, which is dependent on resolution of the EU IVDR compliance issue.  Until this report is received, the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust cannot progress to the opening of the trial. I understand that discussions are ongoing between the HSC Approvals Service, the trial sponsor and the MHRA to resolve the regulatory requirements in NI for the trial.However,  I am pleased to share that the Northern Ireland Cancer Network’s Upper Gastrointestinal Clinical Reference Group are reviewing clinical evidence, including guidance from NICE and the British Society of Gastroenterology, alongside research findings, to determine the most clinically appropriate contexts for potential implementation of capsule sponge testing across the region.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA Bill 15/22-27) lists 11 relevant institutions, but leaves additions to The Executive Office regulations, how they will ensure any decisions in relation to this are transparent and include survivor consultation and Assembly oversight, rather than ministerial discretion.
answer_text: We deeply regret the pain and suffering of victims and survivors and this legislation is an important stage in uncovering the truth and holding those responsible to account. The Bill provides a clear focus for the inquiry and redress scheme but, crucially, it also embeds some flexibility.  There are 11 institutions included in the standardised payment. However, institutions can be added or removed and the time period can be amended, subject to Assembly approval.  For the public inquiry, there will be a prescribed list of institutions, also subject to Assembly approval.  This flexibility is important as it will allow recommendations from the Independent Panel, Inquiry Chair and others to be considered.This ensures democratic scrutiny, but it also means the inquiry and redress scheme can be delivered in a timely way, which is so important, especially for the many birth mothers who have long campaigned for justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what proportion of the new railings on the Whiteabbey seafront came from the (i) active travel budget; and (ii) the roads budget.
answer_text: I can advise that the cost of the new railings at Whiteabbey seafront was funded from my Department’s active travel budget.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the £220,000 allocation to address issues around racial and social cohesion can be targeted to ensure support reaches areas most affected by recent racially motivated incidents.
answer_text: Challenges around racial and social cohesion are being experienced globally. While issues around housing and local services are separate, it is important to state that the Executive strongly condemns sectarianism and racism in any form.Following the disorder in June, we moved quickly to ensure that £220,000 in additional funding was made available through the District Council Good Relations Programme, to help support racial and social cohesion here. Utilising the expertise and local knowledge of council officers helped to ensure that funding was targeted in areas that would benefit most from such interventions. Detail on specific projects funded to date are available on request.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the recent racially motivated violence in Ballymena, for their assessment of the impact that a ten year absence of an action plan and budget for the Racial Equality Strategy has had on its delivery.
answer_text: First and foremost, our thoughts and support are with those impacted by the recent disorder. The Executive unequivocally condemns the violence we have witnessed, and we again call for calm across our communities. The Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025 was designed as a cross-departmental initiative that would support the mainstreaming of work to address racial inequalities. It identified eleven key actions to support this. The majority of these have been completed or are being progressed, with some intended to continue throughout the lifetime of the strategy.While significant progress has been made, recent events have highlighted that there is still much more to do.  We also recognise that the landscape has changed significantly since the current Strategy’s creation.Racial inequalities are not confined to a single area, and cannot be tackled by one department alone, or indeed by government working in isolation.Our focus is very much on ensuring full implementation of remaining actions but in parallel, work is ongoing to develop a new strategic approach to racial equality so that collectively we can strive to create a safe, prosperous and welcoming society for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any action being taken to mitigate against the impact of shortages of the drug Creon.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he has taken to secure supplies of the drug Creon which is in short supply for cancer patients in Northern Ireland, especially those patients with pancreatic cancer.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly complex, regulated and global industry; therefore, medicine shortages are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons. Whilst supply issues can’t always be prevented, there are well-established tools and processes to mitigate risks to patients which are agreed UK-wide and locally via medicine shortage management groups.Although Creon® preparations remain available, my Department is aware that the quantities are limited and capped across the UK due to manufacturing constraints until January 2026, which may result in some localised and sporadic supply disruptions.My Department published an updated National Patient Safety Alert on 23 December 2024 regarding PERT products, which provided healthcare professionals with advice and actions to take to ensure patients can continue to access appropriate treatments. Healthcare professionals are also recommended to use the NHS Specialist Pharmacy Service’s (SPS) website including the medicines supply tool that provides up-to-date information on all medicine supply disruptions and a dedicated  page to assist healthcare professionals on prescribing and ordering PERT products- https://www.sps.nhs.uk/articles/prescribing-and-ordering-available-pancreatic-enzyme-replacement-therapies/. PERT products have been added to the UK restricted export and hoarding list and Serious Shortage Protocols for Creon 10,000 and 25,000 capsules have been issued and apply in Northern Ireland (NI). Unlicensed imports have also been sourced from other markets and may be prescribed and supplied where deemed appropriate.Additionally, I wrote to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting the ongoing impacts medicine disruptions are having in NI, including PERT products, and my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK.Any patients impacted by this supply disruption should contact a healthcare professional for advice on actions to take.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the role that the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse has had in supporting victims of childhood abuse.
answer_text: We are pleased to confirm that, following a review undertaken by Professor Alexis Jay, we have decided that the office of the Commissioner should remain in operation for an additional two years, up to December 2027. We have offered Fiona Ryan the opportunity to remain in her role for this time to continue the good work she and her office do in supporting and advocating for Victims and Survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse.Professor Jay’s review found that, although the workload of the Commissioner has reduced with the recent closure of the Redress Scheme to new applications, a short extension would allow time to consider how best to support victims and survivors moving forward. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how proposals within his Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27 will benefit pets and their owners.
answer_text: The Animal Welfare Pathway sets out a range of practical interventions that I intend to bring forward within the lifetime of this Assembly mandate, to help strengthen our current welfare legislation, and to ensure that there are robust initiatives in place, so our beloved pets can receive the care needed for a high quality of life. I am confident that the proposals within the Pathway, which were developed through engagement with key stakeholders in animal welfare, including the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group (NICAWG), will facilitate and deliver improvements to the lives of companion animals throughout Northern Ireland. The proposals include consideration of the evidence on prohibition of aversive training devices, in particular shock collars, enhancements to microchipping requirements, exploration of mandatory cat microchipping, and development of an Animal Welfare and Responsible Dog Ownership Campaign. Work is already underway on a number of reforms, including the recruitment of an Expert Advisory Group to review dog breeding regulations, and the launch of a public consultation on the proposed new rules for the sale and supply of puppies and kittens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to repair or resurface the footpath on the B90 old Carrick Road, from the boundary with Mid and East Antrim to the junction of the Monkstown Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the resurface threshold per mile for roads that have had numerous repairs and potholes.
answer_text: Under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, my Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition and to seek to fulfil this duty, an annual programme of resurfacing and reconstruction works is undertaken, the extent of which is dictated by the availability of funding.On trunk roads, information is routinely gathered and analysed from a variety of specialist machine surveys and visual inspections and this information is used in order to help inform decisions regarding our annual maintenance programmes. On other roads, divisional programmes are prepared on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage and general surface condition surveys as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims. Programmes are subject to continual review and take into account a wide range of factors such as worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, contractor capacity etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current funding allocated for oesophageal and gastric cancer prevention, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is no funding allocated specifically to health and social care trusts for oesophageal and gastric cancer prevention.  In Northern Ireland several organisations contribute to cancer prevention but primary responsibility lies with the Public Health Agency (PHA). The PHA lead on health promotion and disease prevention initiatives including programmes which may reduce cancer risk such as promoting healthy lifestyle choices (e.g. smoking cessation healthy eating and physical activity), implementing awareness campaigns on lifestyle cancer risks, and supporting screening programmes for early detection. The proportion of cancer incidence in Northern Ireland attributable to modifiable lifestyle and environmental factors is estimated to be in the 30% to 40% range, this includes oesophageal and gastric cancers.  A population approach, supported by policy and legislative measures, is required to encourage and support people to live healthier lives and reduce the prevalence of cancer risk factors such as tobacco and alcohol consumption, obesity, lack of physical activity.The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022 – 2032 emphasises cancer prevention as a key element in its approach and highlights increasing public awareness of cancer-related risk factors through specific strategies on tobacco, substance use, and overweight and obesity – including diet and physical exercise.  A Cancer Strategy Progress Report is due for publication in early 2025 which will include further detail against actions.  A new 3-year cancer strategy plan is also under development which will consider the need for prevention work. It is important to note that all Health and Social Care Trusts carry out surveillance for patients with the premalignant condition, Barrett’s oesophagus.  Barrett’s oesophagus can increase risk of oesophageal cancer.  Surveillance focusses on detecting early signs of precancerous changes to the oesophagus in those diagnosed with Barrett’s Oesophagus. Patients have regular endoscopic surveillance with frequency dependent upon the nature of their condition. Work has also commenced to establish the need for targeted surveillance for other gastric premalignant conditions as part of Cancer Strategy Action 11.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of Sir Chris Hoy's diagnosis, whether he will consider lowering the age limit for prostate cancer testing.
answer_text: Screening for prostate cancer is currently not recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). They last considered the evidence for screening for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. Prostate cancer is rare in men under 50 but the risk increases with age. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My Department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the EU proposals for a universal approach on the welfare of dogs and cats and their traceability will impact Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, new EU laws, including the EU proposal for a universal approach on the welfare of dogs and cats and their traceability, no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities. They come under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.While some of the aspects of this European proposal would not be new to Northern Ireland, it is my view that other elements would enhance and supplement our existing laws.I am very mindful of the welfare benefits of tightening breeding rules, microchipping cats and banning shock collars, so I am actively considering the introduction of domestic legislation here, using existing powers.I have written to the UK Government on this matter and they will be keeping a watching brief on developments.

To ask the Minister of Health, given that one in eight women are expected to face breast cancer in their lifetime, (i) for a summary of the options available to women at high risk of breast cancer; and (ii) whether there will be an expansion of screening programs to enable earlier and more frequent access to those with genetic predispositions or other elevated risk factors.
answer_text: for a summary of the options available to women at high risk of breast cancer If a woman thinks she may be at high risk of breast cancer due to family history she can access information on eligibility for genetic testing on the Genetic Medicine Website.   All NI Trusts have Breast Family History resources available on their websites, these are also available on the Northern Ireland Cancer Network website. Breast family history resources | Northern Ireland Cancer Network (hscni.net)For women and families where there is a known inherited genetic change in a high-risk cancer gene, relatives can either request a referral from their GP or self-refer to the Cancer Genetics service to discuss genetic testing. The service offers cascade family testing to blood relatives of the index patient to identify those family members who have inherited the variant and are at a higher risk of developing breast, ovarian, and other cancers. This enables the establishment of preventive and surveillance measures such as risk-reducing mastectomy or chemo prevention treatment. Family history clinics are embedded within the breast service of each trust.   A patient may also be referred by their GP to a family history clinic for assessment due to family history of breast cancer.  Family history clinics identify women who may be eligible for genetic testing or may benefit from a further assessment with the Cancer Genetics Service. Chemo prevention drugs are discussed with all patients at family history clinics in line with National Institute for Health & Care Excellence (NICE) guidance.  Where appropriate patients with familial / genetic risk will be offered risk reduction surgery in all Trusts to reduce the risk of breast cancer. All Trusts have Breast Family History resources available on their websites, they are also available on the Northern Ireland Cancer Network website. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of how to address cancer treatment waiting times, in line with the priorities in the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: Our cancer waiting times are unacceptable. To make sure people wait shorter we need to reform healthcare services to provide our clinicians with the most effective tools to do their job. Elective Care Centres is one way we help to address this issue. These centres of excellence makes surgery outcomes improve and provide a more effective and efficient service.We must also ensure that pathways into cancer services are as streamlined as possible. To this end, reviews of the three main haematological cancer pathways have been completed. This will ensure the best pathways for our patients ensuring faster treatment and better outcomes.We are also leading the way in implementing an optimal care pathway for Pancreatic Cancer – this has the potential to significantly reduce waiting times for suspected pancreatic cancer patients. I hope that this model of work can then be rolled out across other tumour sites reducing waits. My officials are also working with the HSC to establish more resilient and equitable services for patients with breast cancer, and Head and Neck Cancer.The Cancer Strategy establishes a strategic roadmap to improve outcomes for all patients who require cancer treatment and must be fully implemented. While some of this can be achieved through existing resources, additional investment in workforce and capital equipment is essential if we are to increase capacity to meet service demands.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the draft Refugee Integration Strategy.
answer_text: We are considering a draft Refugee Integration Strategy and plan to bring a final version, and an associated Thematic Delivery Plan, to the Executive for agreement shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timeline on the implementation of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy is a 10 year plan to reform cancer services in Northern Ireland. Since publication in 2022, we have already seen significant progress against a number of actions. Key examples of progress to date include the development of Rapid Diagnosis Centres and services to provide genomic testing for cancer patients. Both these services will help improve pathways and improve patient outcomes.We have completed reviews of haematological cancer pathways, and Adolescent and Young Adult cancer services, and are progressing work to implement the recommendations.We have invested in oncology and haematology stabilisation plans and Clinical Nurse Specialists, to ensure that the services are better staffed.Work is currently underway to review Breast Cancer Services and to implement an optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer patients. The focus of this work is reducing the length of time that these patients are waiting for diagnosis and treatment, therefore improving patient outcomes.Children’s oncology and haematology services have benefited from closer collaboration with our closest neighbours, including the development of bone marrow stem cell transplant services.We are developing a cancer research strategy which will be published this year which will help increase the uptake in clinical trials, therefore improving outcomes.There is undoubtedly more to be done, and additional investment is required, but the cancer strategy is already starting to deliver benefits for cancer patients in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Clinical Oncology Workforce Census, to detail his plans to ensure the 9 per cent shortfall of consultant oncologists (i) is addressed; and (ii) does not impact patient safety or the quality of care.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the workforce challenges that exists across health and social care and therefore welcome the Royal College of Radiologists’ 2023 Workforce Census Reports for Clinical Oncology and Clinical Radiology.  I have already agreed to meet representatives from the Royal College to discuss their recommendations in detail, including their predictions relating to retirements and shortfalls in the current and future number of clinical oncologists and clinical/interventional radiologists.  This will inform my Department’s ongoing collaboration with the health and social care system on the development of imaging and cancer services.  My Department is committed to the provision of effective imaging and cancer services in Northern Ireland, ensuring patient safety and quality of care.  The Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in Health and Social Care (2018) recognised that our existing training mechanism could not deliver the number of clinical radiologists required to meet increasing demand.  While much has been achieved over recent years, with training places having been increased by 46% from 37 (2015/16) to 54 (2022/23), the Department recognises that a more innovative approach is required to generate the radiologist workforce required to meet future demand.After extensive consideration of best practice options, including those set out in the Royal College of Radiology’s Radiology Training 2016 – 2026: A vision and a solution, the Department and HSC are taking forward a proposal for a multi-professional Imaging Academy for Northern Ireland.  This will significantly increase the supply of locally trained consultant radiologists and advanced radiographer practitioners to address the increasing demand for imaging investigations and reduce reliance on costly outsourcing.Following a robust options appraisal process by an independent expert panel, the Mater Infirmorum Hospital site in Belfast HSC Trust has been identified as the optimum location for the proposed Imaging Academy. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is collaborating with other UK jurisdictions that have banned sunbeds in order to learn from their experiences and follow best practices.
answer_text: Whilst commercial sunbed bans have been introduced in some countries including Australia and Brazil, UK and European jurisdictions have typically introduced age restrictions and other regulatory measures in relation to their use. The Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy steering group frequently engage with other jurisdictions in relation to best practice and the Department  continues to work with partner organisations, including the Public Health Agency, District Councils and Cancer Focus NI, to reduce sunbed use and raise awareness of the harms they cause.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken to ensure that the public is adequately informed and consulted with in relation to proposed Battery Energy Storage Systems installations, especially those near residential areas, to address any community concerns.
answer_text: Provisions in the Planning Act (NI) 2011, the Planning (General Development Procedure) Order (NI) 2015 (‘GDPO’) and the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 provide for pre application community consultation to be undertaken for applications for development belonging to the category of major development. Consultation includes at least one public event and notice in a newspaper. Regulation 8 of the GDPO makes provision for notice to be given on receipt of applications for planning permission. It includes advertisement in local newspapers and neighbour notification by letter to prescribed properties.Members of the public can also find and follow planning applications via the Planning Portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the recent rise in installations of Battery Energy Storage Systems, what steps he is taking to develop a specific regulatory framework that addresses the fire safety and environmental risks associated with these systems.
answer_text: Through an existing framework which includes planning policies; local development plans; guidance; environmental impact assessments; case law; pre-application community consultation; pre-application discussions; consultation with expert bodies and consideration at planning committee meetings I am content that the risks associated with these developments and any separation distances from residential properties can be addressed at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the guidance provided to local council planning decision-makers to ensure that battery installations include comprehensive decommissioning plans, covering end-of-life management and environmentally sound disposal of batteries.
answer_text: Guidance on a range of topics has been provided by my Department through Development Management Practice Notes. These are designed to guide planning officers and others engaged in the planning system through development management processes and deal primarily with procedures as well as good practice. From April 2019 my department has engaged with local planning authorities (LPAs) as part of its Environmental Governance Work Programme, intended to enhance competence and capacity on environmental compliance in the planning system, particularly in relation to environmental impact assessment (EIA). The programme included the development and delivery of two levels of bespoke EIA training to over 200 planning staff, the publication of a number of EIA Development Management Practice Notes and the establishment of an Environmental Officers Forum, to facilitate discussions on key and emerging EIA issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how current safety regulations for Battery Energy Storage Systems compare with Regulation (EU) 2023/1542; and (ii) whether there are any plans to align standards with these regulations.
answer_text: Regulation (EU) 2023/1542, which amends Directive 2008/98/EC and Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 and repeals Directive 2006/66/EC, has been in force from 18th February 2024 and is applicable here.  It replaces the EU Batteries Directive (2006/66/EC), which is listed in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework.It will gradually introduce new rules to strengthen sustainability of batteries and waste batteries, to protect the environment and human health, and increase circularity.The North of Ireland will implement this EU regulation as it is within the scope of the Windsor Framework. DfE is currently assessing the necessary requirements and supporting implementing legislation required and is working closely with DAERA.  DfE will engage with stakeholders through this process, however the expected timeline is not known at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the evidence linking sunbed use to increased melanoma risk, what steps he is taking to ban sunbeds in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department’s Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy aims to reduce the incidence of skin cancer and therefore future regulatory measures in relation to sunbeds have not been ruled out. We continue to work with partner organisations, including the Public Health Agency, District Councils and Cancer Focus NI, to reduce sunbed use and raise awareness of the harms they cause.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the action his Department is taking in relation to blue-green algae in Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the role of affordable childcare in addressing poverty.
answer_text: I appreciate the role which appropriate early learning and childcare provision can play in helping children and families out of poverty.  The Department for Communities leads on the Anti-Poverty Strategy and is represented on the Task and Finish Group which I established to support the development of an Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  Their input has helped shape the package of measures which I announced on 23 May.  This includes a commitment to increase investment in early years programmes such as Sure Start, Pathway and Toybox.  These are targeted at some of the most disadvantaged communities and groups within Northern Ireland and aim to ‘level the playing field’ for many young children experiencing poverty.  Research by the Institute of Fiscal Studies shows that child development outcomes are most effectively achieved through group programmes like this which provide a range of wrap-around support and include parental involvement.In addition, families on Universal Credit are entitled to significant support with their childcare costs which particularly benefits parents working fewer hours and on lower incomes.  In the 3 years from December 2020 to November 2023, 185,000 Universal Credit childcare payments were made in Northern Ireland, totalling £80m.   I am aware of the financial strain which childcare costs are putting on many families not eligible for Universal Credit.  That is why I have decided to introduce a Northern Ireland childcare subsidy scheme initially targeted at parents eligible for Tax Free Childcare who have children below Primary school age.  This group tends to be the highest users of childcare, incur the greatest costs and are most likely to consider it ‘difficult or very difficult to afford’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will answer AQW 11532/22-27.
answer_text: The response to AQW 11532 22-27 was issued on 22 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he was aware of planning application LA03/2022/1109/F for the redevelopment of 570 to 578 Shore Road, Newtownabbey, when considering the safety measures for the Glenville Road and A2 junction.
answer_text: I can confirm my officials were aware of, and have taken into account, planning application LA03/2022/1109/F in their consideration of safety measures at the junction of Glenville Road with the A2 Shore Road, Whiteabbey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he will ensure the additional 600 vehicle movements daily from the planning application LA03/2022/1109/F, concentrated during peak travel periods, does not exacerbate the existing safety issues at the A2 and Glenville Road junction, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Planning application LA03/2022/1109/F for a community pharmacy and residential apartments is a current 'live' Planning Application which falls under the consideration and governance of Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council Planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) current; and (ii) future plans to support the development of offshore wind programs.
answer_text: In order to meet the targets established in the Climate Change Act and the Energy Strategy, our energy system must diversify to harness an increasing amount of indigenous and clean energy sources. Offshore renewable energy will have a key role to play in achieving net zero.My Department, in collaboration with other departments and key stakeholders, have developed an Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) to enable offshore renewable energy development in the north’s marine area from 2030. Department officials are also developing a support scheme to incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects. The High-Level Design for this support scheme was published in April 2024. I recognise the importance of offshore renewables for our net zero future. Incentivising the necessary investment in offshore projects will continue to be part of the development process for the support scheme, in line with the work on the critical path timeline for offshore renewable energy being undertaken by the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools in East Antrim awaiting the completion of capital projects; (ii) the progress, status, and anticipated completion dates of these projects; and (iii) the current prioritisation ranking of these projects.
answer_text: There are currently seven capital projects in East Antrim awaiting completion.The current position of these projects is as follows:School Current Position Islandmagee Primary School Project is in the construction stage, with completion anticipated in summer 2024.St Killian’s CollegeProject is in the design stage and a revised planning application is to be submitted in the coming months. Belfast High SchoolThe project was announced in March 2022, however was paused in April 2023.Carrickfergus AcademyProcurement of an Integrated Consultant Team for the project has commenced, following the re-start of the project in February 2024.Larne Grammar SchoolThe project was announced in March 2022, however was paused in April 2023.Larne High School The project was announced in March 2022, however was paused in April 2023.Ulidia Integrated CollegeProject is in the construction stage with completion anticipated by the end of 2024. Projects will continue to advance in planning and design but will only be permitted to progress to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.  As I have previously stated, it is my intention to continue to make the case for significant additional and sustained capital budget to invest right across the schools’ estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on a new domestic and sexual abuse strategy.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what action she is taking to bring forward social value legislation.
answer_text: Leveraging public spend to achieve better social value outcomes is a key priority. To date positive outcomes of the Scorning Social Value Procurement Policy Note have included: • 3,500+ people who were long-term unemployed or disadvantaged in the labour market have gained employment • 75+ social enterprises are now part of government supply chains • 2,700+ hours of volunteering with the Voluntary, Community and Social Enterprises sector • 6,500+ hours of skills development activities • 2,800+ weeks of work experience • Over 3,500 hours of environmental awareness initiatives • 2400+ hours of health and well-being initiativesThe new Procurement Act 2023 places a duty on contracting authorities to have regard ‘to the importance of maximising public benefit’ and ‘to the fact that small and medium-sized enterprises may face particular barriers to participation and consider whether such barriers can be removed or reduced’.In light of the new regulations, I am also reviewing the current Scoring Social Value Policy to identify areas where the Policy can be strengthened to improve its impact. One of the issues I am keen to address is feedback received from representative groups for small and medium sized and micro businesses who believe that some areas of the Scoring Social Value Policy may be impacting on their ability to tender for government contracts. The ability to amend Policy, particularly to address any unintended consequences, is essential. My priority at present is to ensure we have the most impactful means to deliver Social Value. Following consultation with the key stakeholders, I plan to bring the revised Policy to the Executive for approval following the Summer recess.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any steps they have taken to implement the recommendations and proposals of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition Final Report, since 3 February 2024.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any progress that has been made to provide traffic controlling measures at the junction between Shore Road and Glenville Road, Whiteabbey.
To ask the Minister of Health, given that it is 50 years old, whether he intends to replace the Tower Block facilities at the City Hospital.
answer_text: Construction of the 15 storey Tower Block at the Belfast City Hospital commenced in the 1970s with the Tower Block opening on 6th January 1986.  The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have submitted a proposal to carry out refurbishment of the Belfast City Hospital Tower Block and to date funding of £6.4m has been provided to the Trust.  Funding will continue to be provided to the Trust on an ongoing annual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Northern Ireland will contribute to the National Cancer Diagnosis Audit.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with clinicians, and the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, to determine what can be done to include Northern Ireland data within the National Cancer Diagnosis Audit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why PatientView has ceased to operate in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The UK Kidney Association has agreed a new arrangement with ‘Patients Knows Best’ to re-platform their patient facing system ‘Patient View’ across the UK.This has involved a period of transition to allow Trusts to undertake Cyber Security and Data Protection Impact Assessments consistent with digital governance requirements.  These are now complete.The UK Kidney Association and Patient Knows Best have been contacted and advised of the imminence of roll out in Northern Ireland. A registration email is to be forwarded to all patients who have consented to the previous Renal Patient View System. Patients will be able to follow this link and sign up online giving them access to available digital health records. We hope the information above assures you that we are making good progress to enable access to Patient Knows Best for people in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what steps he is taking to ensure Special Educational Needs schools are fully resourced to deal with the rising demand for school places.
answer_text: Department budgets for 2024-25 were recently agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive.  Over the past number of years, Special Educational Needs (SEN) costs have continued to increase year-on-year, with an anticipated c£156m required in 2024-25 to meet increasing demand and to take forward a Transformation Programme for SEN. As part of the department's 2024-25 budget bids, I have sought the necessary funding required to ensure that special schools and the wider system are effectively resourced for this incoming academic year. The full amount of funding sought has not been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of cycle accidents in each of the last five years, broken down by age profile.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information. The PSNI are responsible for collecting and validating data on Road Traffic Collisions.  I would, therefore, refer you to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when a review of the Cycle Proficiency Scheme was last carried out.
answer_text: The Cycling Proficiency Scheme was externally reviewed in 2013.  The Department subsequently updated the Scheme and associated resources in 2015 which brought it broadly in line with cycle training across the UK. The current Cycling Proficiency Scheme sits between National Standards levels 1 and 2, with the main difference being that on-road training is an option for schools to consider at the end of the scheme. The ongoing effectiveness of the scheme is also monitored on an annual basis by Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) and a report card produced to document the results. The Cycling Proficiency Scheme is offered to all primary schools and has consistently been supported and valued. I’m pleased to say that, since this year’s scheme was launched last month, the level of interest from schools wishing to participate is extremely encouraging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many primary schools avail of the Cycle Proficiency Scheme.
answer_text: The Cycling Proficiency Scheme was delivered in 314 schools in 2022/23. There was a drop in numbers in 2023/24 to 150 schools due largely to a reduction in funding available for the scheme.  I have recently confirmed that the scheme will be funded in 2024/25 and I’m pleased to say that to date 258 schools have indicated their intention to offer the scheme to their pupils this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the April 2024 vasectomy services announcement, how he intends to assist GP practices in the resulting increased appointments.
answer_text: I was delighted to announce that non-scalpel vasectomy services in Northern Ireland will be transferred from secondary care to primary care.  This will take effect from April 2024, with a transition phase throughout 2024/25.  This service enables GPs to refer patients requesting a non-scalpel vasectomy to a designated host practice where an accredited GP Vasectomist will perform the procedure negating the need for a referral to secondary care. Funding has been committed to support the provision of this procedure across Primary Care Elective and I can advise that work has commenced with the SPPG and the Federation Support Unit to re-establish this service for patients across Northern Ireland with a transition phase throughout 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9059/22-27, how many rectification notices have been served in the last five years.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads to an agreed specification. When utility companies do not reinstate roads in a satisfactory manner, the Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections. The number of reinstatement failures is set out in the table below.
