question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what cancer prevention vaccines are available; and (ii) the delivery programme of each vaccine.
answer_text: The only cancer preventable vaccine available in Northern Ireland and the rest of the UK, is the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine, which primarily prevents cervical cancer.  It also helps reduce some types of other cancers linked to HPV, such as head and neck cancer, including oropharyngeal cancer (a type of throat cancer) and mouth cancer.    The HPV vaccine is offered routinely, through a school-based vaccination programme, to all males and females aged 12 to 13 years (school Year 9).It is also available to people up to the age of 25 who, for whatever reason, did not take up the offer of vaccination in school, on request via their GP.In addition, HPV vaccination is also offered to gay, bisexual and other men who have sex with men, and some transgender people, up to the age of 45, via specialist sexual-health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why full grant funding is not available for students choosing to complete degrees online.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. I have asked my officials to update me on any work to date on a review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward.  As part of this, I will be engaging with my officials to consider the current support available and to determine whether this can be improved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the encompass programme will enable cancer patients and survivors to have virtual access to their own digital clinical data.
answer_text: encompass, which went live in the South Eastern Trust on 9 November 2023 and will be implemented region wide by Spring next year, includes a patient portal called My Care.  This portal enables patients and service users to take more control over their care. Information such as letters, supporting information, lab results and radiology results will be made available to patients. To ensure that support is in place for patients in dealing with sensitive or upsetting information, some information relating to cancer testing and diagnosis will not be available immediately through the portal. This information will not be released until the patient has had an in-person discussion with their care team.A separate regional information system (RISOH) will continue to be used for detailed documentation of oncology treatments until a replacement system is in place.  RISOH and encompass have been interfaced to enable patients to see the treatment appointment details in My Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the standards utility providers must meet when carrying out road repairs.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order) forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers such as utility providers. The Order requires the undertaker, when reinstating a street, to comply with such requirements as may be prescribed in relation to the specification of materials and the standards of workmanship to be observed. These requirements have been prescribed in the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition), a code of practice approved by the Department under Article 31 of the Order. Details of this can be found at:www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/nirauc-code-of-practice-specification-for-the-reinstatement-of-openings-in-roads-2nd-edition.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that 66,000 people in Northern Ireland are living with prediabetes, what support his Department is providing to the Type 2 Diabetes Prevention Programme in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Diabetes Prevention Programme is an evidence-based diabetes prevention programme for NI, delivered by the Public Health Agency (PHA). The overall aim of the programme is to reduce the incidence of Type 2 Diabetes by identifying individuals at high risk and offering referral to an evidence-based behaviour change programme. This regional programme was commissioned and implemented by PHA, across all five Heath and Social Care Trusts. While this programme was not recurrently funded, HSCTs continue to deliver at a reduced volume.PHA leads on the non-departmental actions of the “Fitter Future for All” 10-year Obesity Prevention framework 2012 - 22. PHA invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes that align to diabetes prevention and continues to work with a range of other partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether the road repairs carried out by a utility provider on the A2 Road from Trooperslane to Hazelbank roundabout are of a sufficient standard.
answer_text: Reinstatements made by utility companies have to meet the required Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My officials have carried out various inspections during the works, which has already led to remedial works being undertaken by the utility company.  Further inspections will be carried out jointly with departmental officials and the utility company, and the findings of the independent report you previously provided will also be raised, ensuring that works are carried out safely and to the appropriate standards.Utility works are also subject to additional inspection regimes on completion of the works, along with routine road inspections, which will ensure that any defective work can be passed onto the utility company for rectification during the appropriate warranty period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any conversations his Department has had with the Department for the Economy in supporting social enterprises by providing affordable and accessible childcare.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is represented on the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy Programme Board, the Stakeholder Engagement Forum and the Task and Finish Group contributing to the development of short and longer-term support measures.  Delivery of an ambitious strategy will require the involvement of all types of provider, including social enterprises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of apprenticeships available in East Antrim.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes are demand-led, with apprentices recruited and retained by employers in line with business needs. As published in the latest ApprenticeshipsNI statistical bulletin, 438 apprentices in East Antrim were enrolled on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme as at July 2022.		In relation to the Higher Level Apprenticeships programme, in the academic year 2021/2022, a total of 63 apprentices in East Antrim were undertaking higher level apprenticeships, of which 28 were enrolled at a Further Education college (including the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise), and 35 were enrolled at a Northern Ireland higher education institution.It should be noted that Parliamentary Constituency is based on an apprentice’s home postcode. Although the apprentice may live in a particular Parliamentary Constituency, they may attend work elsewhere.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assistance is available to support schools to travel to Parliament Buildings for an official visit.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to make no-fault divorces available in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am keen to engage with others who may be able to make contributions in relation to no-fault divorces.I have had exploratory discussions with my officials in the Department who are engaged on a range of civil law reform areas including marriage and civil partnership reform and other important policies. I will consider this issue within the available resources and overall legislative priorities, and officials will begin preparatory work with the aim to see progress on this policy area during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6323/22-27, what chemotherapy treatments are available on a cross-border basis at Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Trust (North West Cancer Centre) has a Service Level Agreement in place with HSE for the provision of radiotherapy to ROI patients and the provision of concurrent radiotherapy/chemotherapy for a small number of patients (as clinically indicated).Therefore, no stand-alone chemotherapy treatment is provided at NWCC for ROI patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the UK government’s launch of the Children and Young People Cancer Taskforce, what action he is taking to ensure children and young people in Northern Ireland with cancer have similar treatment.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy recognises the need for a tailored service for children and young people with cancer. My Department is developing minimum standards for treatment of Adolescents and Young Adults with cancer. This seeks to ensure that all children and young adults have access to high quality, developmentally appropriate, cancer care, including access to clinical trials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any proposals to introduce legislation that would make it unlawful for compensation to be paid in respect of a child, without court approval in minor settlements.
answer_text: Further to the consultation in 2021 on this issue, my Department has been considering the detail of the provision that would be needed to introduce a new statutory requirement for court approval of all minor settlements. This has highlighted a number of complex legal and practical issues, including upon whom any duty to seek court approval should be placed, how such a duty could be enforced and the consequences of non-compliance. My Department is currently considering whether there is a legislative solution, which is feasible and proportionate, having regard also to the existing position, under the common law, that a settlement for a child which is not approved by a court is not binding and final. If any legislative provision is appropriate, it is my intention that, subject to Justice Committee and Executive agreement, this could be facilitated by way of inclusion in the mixed-content Modernisation of Justice Bill, which is one of my legislative priorities in this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what discussions have taken place with the Housing Executive to support leaseholders who are struggling to afford planned maintenance and refurbishment scheme costs as ordered by the Housing Executive under the right-to-buy scheme; and (ii) what action he has taken to create a sinking fund similar to what happens in the private sector given that no adequate facility is currently in place.
answer_text: My officials have been working with the Housing Executive (NIHE) for some time, not only on the matter of supporting leaseholders but also in relation to the fact that if a number of leaseholders are not able to pay for the planned works this has the potential to block much needed work to NIHE tenanted properties. With this in mind, DfC and DoF have given approval to the Housing Executive to offer buy back with vacant possession option to leaseholders if the cost of the works is over £10k.When delivering Planned Scheme works, Housing Executive (NIHE) advises leaseholders of the estimated costs of works in advance of commencement of the scheme on site.  The NIHE also offers leaseholders the opportunity to pay into a Planned Scheme account for these works, in advance of receiving the final invoice when works are completed.  I can advise that the Housing Executive is continuing to consider the issue of leasehold properties.  The Housing Executive has advised the standard Flat Lease by virtue of which all Housing Executive flats/maisonettes are sold under the statutory House Sales Scheme, empowers but does not compel the Housing Executive to create a reserve to finance future capital costs. When the sale of flats commenced around 1983 the Housing Executive did not elect to create such a reserve and no contributions to such a reserve have ever been included in the Service Charge which is collected from Leaseholders. The Housing Executive does not currently have any proposals to ask Leaseholders to contribute to the creation of a reserve fund for those of its blocks which contain sold flats.  In the short to mid-term there are already major planned scheme works scheduled, which would mean Leaseholders would have to make a contribution towards the cost of future potential works at the same time as having to make payment for works that have already been carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline when Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding will be available for sports clubs.
answer_text: My officials have been working closely with clubs at all levels to build strong relationships and understand needs and are developing proposals in partnership with an Advisory Working Group.I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration to take this commitment forward as a matter of priority. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given news that thousands of patients in England at risk of bowel cancer will be offered regular checks, what action he is taking to detect cancers early in people with Lynch syndrome.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, patients with a family history of colorectal cancer can access colonoscopy surveillance as recommended by British Society of Gastroenterology. Assessment of familial colorectal cancer risk is available to patients via the Northern Ireland Regional Genetics Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to implement Martha’s Rule in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the recent announcement by NHS England in respect of Martha’s Rule and will take time to consider the plans for rollout of the scheme in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his engagement with pre-school education settings on their readiness to facilitate the extension of the Pre-School Education Programme.
To ask the Minister of Health, given the impact of the prolonged impasse, what action he is planning to take to fast track the Cancer Strategy in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The implementation of the NI Cancer strategy remains a priority for my Department since its launch in March 2022.  Despite the many challenges presented over the proceeding years, considerable progress has been made by my officials within the constrained budgetary resources available. The prioritisation of the cancer strategy will be considered along with the many other compelling but competing demands for extra funding across the health and social care system at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many oncology patients from County Donegal receive treatment in Altnagelvin Hospital; and (ii) what treatments are available to these patients.
answer_text: Cross Border arrangements have been in place with Republic of Ireland (ROI) since 2017 for radiotherapy services.  A total of 1353 ROI Oncology patients have received radiotherapy treatment at the North West Cancer Centre (NWCC), Altnagelvin Hospital.  This figure includes 268 patients treated during the calendar year 2023.  Patients are funded by the Health Services Executive through a service level agreement with Western HSC Trust.NWCC offers treatment for palliative patients across all tumour sites and radical treatments for Breast, Prostate, Lung, Gastrointestinal and Skin.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what his plans are in relation to the agri-food sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 1164/22-27; (ii) AQW 3304/22-27; and (iii) AQW 3305/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health what actions his Department is taking to assist social work students in full time unpaid placement, in light of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department provides a Social Work Student Incentive Scheme for all  NI Degree in Social Work students who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study.  In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements.  The Student Incentive Scheme is in addition to any Student Finance support, which can be applied for separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department has taken to meet the requirements of the Climate Change Act.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – The Path to Net Zero Energy, has committed to the vision of net zero carbon by 2050. It outlines a roadmap to 2030 aiming to deliver a 56% reduction in energy-related emissions, which will contribute to the 48% lower than baseline target in net Northern Ireland emissions required by the Climate Change Act by 2030.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what action he has taken to further progress the Energy Strategy; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact a vacancy in the ministerial office post 28 October 2022 will have on the Strategy.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – The Path to Net Zero Energy, continues to progress since its publication in December 2021.  Delivery of the Energy Strategy is governed by the Energy Strategy Programme Board, which is chaired by DfE’s Permanent Secretary and consists of members from DfC, DfI, DAERA, DoF and TEO. The Energy Strategy Programme board has met 3 times in 2022.		Monthly checkpoint meetings are held involving the leads for the delivery of the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022, which has 22 key Actions for priority attention during this calender year.  My Department has committed to publish a report in Q1 of 2023 to demonstrate progress against the 22 Actions, as well as ongoing work to publish an Action Plan for 2023 to further progress the Energy Strategy.		Should there not be a Minister in place after 28th October 2022, senior officials will continue to carry out departmental functions and run public services, operating within the scope of the policy direction set by the outgoing Minister.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the strategies or actions his Department has taken to ensure all citizens have the opportunity to become digitally literate and fully participate in our increasingly digital society.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) all of the organisations in East Antrim at risk of losing EU Funding; and (ii) what action he is taking to reduce the resulting impact.
answer_text: I recognise the valuable contribution many local groups in East Antrim, indeed right across Northern Ireland, make to our communities. I plan to make an announcement shortly on the funding of all ESF groups for the remainder of this financial year.Details of current ESF supported projects, for whom EU funding will cease on 31 March 2023, can be accessed on a constituency-by-constituency basis on-line as follows: 65 European Social Fund Call 3 Projects by constituency and council area | Department for the Economy (economy-ni.gov.uk)ERDF funding will cease on 31 December 2023. East Antrim based organisations currently in receipt of ERDF funding through Invest NI are as follows: Advanced Sensors Limited & Teva NI Limited.The Finance Minister continues to take responsibility on behalf of the NI Executive for replacement of all EU funding. I continue to strongly advocate for funding to be directed to key priority areas which includes activity currently funded through the European Social & Regional Development Funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline (i) the impact that the absence of an Executive has on the development of policy and legislation; and (ii) what measures he can take to minimise the impact.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3396/22-27, to outline the number of electric vehicles expected to be in use for each year.
answer_text: In developing the Transport input to the Executive’s Energy Strategy the Department for Infrastructure commissioned four research projects, one of which, Development of Electric Vehicles in Northern Ireland was produced by Steer.  In its report, Steer forecasts there could be between 60,000 and 125,000 EVs in the North by 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3306/22-27, what steps he is taking to support taxi drivers in the short to medium term.
answer_text: You are aware of the recent public consultation on increasing the maximum fare structure. The consultation was open for eight weeks and closed on the 7 October 2022.  My officials are now preparing a synopsis of the consultation responses and a paper on the way forward for my consideration. It is only after this process has concluded that my decision on the outcome of the review can be implemented along with any necessary amendments to the Taxis (Taximeters, Devices and Maximum Fares) Regulations (NI) 2015. This review is to help balance the needs of drivers, operators and taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers.You may wish to note that, the training manual, “All you need to know to be a Taxi Driver”, which is available on NIDirect, is under review to create a more streamlined, user-friendly version. As a result a revision aid has been developed, based on the information contained in the training manual.  This revision aid will soon be available through NIDirect and will help to support candidates in acquiring the knowledge needed for the taxi theory test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps he is taking to support university students during the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I acknowledge the impact the cost of living is having on households across Northern Ireland.In recognition of the cost of living crisis and the impact this is having on higher education students, for the current academic year, I have been able to allocate additional funding of £2.8m to the Higher Education Support Funds which are administered on my Department's behalf by the five local higher education institutions. The Support Funds provide financial help to students whose access to higher education might be inhibited by financial considerations, or who, for whatever reason, face financial difficulties in meeting their living costs.This additional funding brings the total allocation for the year to £5.6m. My Department has provided greater flexibility for the local higher education institutions, when dealing with student applications, to ensure that any student who is experiencing genuine financial difficulty has access to support. 	I have also approved an allocation of £1.17m to provide a further 10% increase, in addition to the already planned 2.9% uplift, to the non-repayable stipend to cover DfE funded Post Graduate Scheme student’s living costs in academic year 2022/23.  This increase is designed to help mitigate against the impact of the increased cost of living for PhD students, particularly those from a lower socio-economic background.  The increase means that 729 Full Time Equivalent DfE-funded PhD students commencing or continuing their studies in October 2022, will see their annual living cost stipend rise from £16,062 in academic year 2021/22, to £17,668 academic year 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what actions he is taking to support the social enterprise sector in 2022/2023.
answer_text: It is important to note from the outset that, in April 2015, as part of the transfer of functions under the Review of Public Administration (RPA), responsibility for social entrepreneurship transferred from the then Department of Enterprise, Trade and Investment (DETI) to local government.   My Department’s support for the sector is primarily through the funding of the Social Economy Work Programme (SEWP) which is delivered by Social Enterprise NI (SENI). One of the SEWP’s key objectives is to develop a cohesive approach to procurement opportunities, training, lobbying and working with central government, councils, education bodies and other groups.  My Department has also included an action for SENI as part of the SEWP to develop a database of the actual social enterprises operational within NI. The database, which will be the intellectual property of DfE, is designed to capture the contact details, location, size, capacity and offering of each individual social enterprise.  This work is ongoing.In addition, my Department’s Skills Focus and InnovateUs programmes have been extended to enable social enterprises to apply for support.Invest NI has advised that the Northern Ireland Business Start Up Programme (NIBSUP), promoted under the ‘Go for it’ brand and delivered by Councils in Northern Ireland, is available to all potential new start businesses, including social enterprises. The programme provides one-to-one support from experienced business advisors, with help to develop a practical business plan and referral to follow on support options.Invest NI also provides a comprehensive range of information on www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk which is particularly helpful for people considering starting a social enterprise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many electric vehicle charging points will be available by the end of (i) 2022; (ii) 2023; (iii) 2024; and (iv) 2025.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network. ESB can be contacted directly regarding its plans for its charge point infrastructure. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. Derry City and Strabane District council is best placed to advise about the application. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to discourage and reduce the use of disposable vaping products among children and young people.
answer_text: As you will be aware I introduced regulations on 1 February prohibiting the sale, and proxy purchase, of nicotine inhaling products to under 18s. These regulations are enforced by district council enforcement officers and apply to all types of nicotine containing e-cigarettes, including disposable devices. The Public Health Agency (PHA) commissions a smoking prevention uptake programme for children and young people called 'Smoke Busters' which includes information on the harms associated with vaping.  The programme is facilitated by Cancer Focus NI and is delivered in primary schools across NI, targeting 6,000 Year 6 and 7 pupils each year.  In addition, the PHA are exploring (in liaison with CCEA) opportunities for a post primary education programme regarding vaping, as well as the development of age appropriate resources warning of the harms associated with vaping, that can be accessed by schools, community youth groups and parents/carers.Recent reports from other parts of the UK indicate a rise in youth vaping. The Department will continue to monitor this position and will consider appropriate mechanisms to reduce youth uptake as part of our strategic work on tobacco control.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to review access to regular scans for patients who have been treated for primary breast cancer.
answer_text: A review has recently been completed on the Breast Cancer Self Directed After Care Protocol and Survivorship Pathway.  This will be presented for ratification at the next Northern Ireland Cancer Network Breast Clinical Reference Group meeting 21 October 2022.  The Breast Cancer Self Directed After Care Protocol and Survivorship Pathway is offered to patients who are treated with curative intent.Access to scans currently aligns with NICE guideline 101 (NG101 Early and locally advanced breast cancer: diagnosis and management).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3081/22-27, to outline all the categories of patients who are entitled to electricity reimbursement for costs associated with their condition.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3081/22-27, what energy support is available for patients living with (i) cancer; and (ii) a terminal illness.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 3079/22-27, what factors contributed to his assessment that a statutory right to time off work for people undergoing fertility treatment is not a priority at this time.
answer_text: Many factors, including financial; legal; resource; and time inform the prioritisation of policy making and implementation. In the present case, my Department is under a statutory obligation to introduce regulations which will deliver a statutory right to miscarriage leave and pay and domestic abuse safe leave.In the case of miscarriage leave and pay, regulations must be introduced by my Department no later than 6 April 2026. I gave a commitment during the last Assembly mandate I would endeavour to do so by April 2024. I remain committed to achieving this target.The Domestic Abuse (Safe Leave) Act (NI) 2022 commenced life as a private members Bill. Much of the policy development work which would otherwise have been undertaken by a departmentally sponsored bill before it undergoes the legislative process and receives Royal Assent now needs to be undertaken. In both instances, stakeholder engagement and formal consultation will be required. It is for these reasons that I have asked officials to prioritise these important pieces of work at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to support taxi drivers, given the rising cost of fuel.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North. This role requires balancing the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   My Department remains committed to working within this remit to play its part, alongside those in the taxi industry, to address the range of issues the industry faces, including the high cost of fuel. As part of its regulatory role the Department is currently consulting with the public, taxi drivers/operators and stakeholders on a review of taxi fares, which will finish on the 7th of October 2022 -  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/consultations/taxi-fare-increase-public-consultation-2022 . This review has factored in a large increase for depot charges, 2022 fuel prices, and other increased costs such as insurance and labour.   We are actively encouraging all of those with an interest to respond to the consultation, and I would ask that you respond if you have not already done so, and also encourage anyone that has raised the issue with you to respond by the deadline.  After this process has concluded the outcome of the review can be implemented, to help address the needs of drivers, operators and taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what consideration has been given to removing plastic bottles from sale in the canteen in Parliament Buildings, and switching to more sustainable packaging.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is committed to being an exemplar organisation in terms of sustainability and works closely with its support services contractor, Sodexo, to achieve this.As part of its continuing certification under ISO 14001:2015 (the International Standard for Environmental Management Systems), the Assembly Commission develops and monitors progress against a Continuous Improvement Plan for each year. The Plan for 2022-23 includes the commitment to “Continue to work towards phasing out single use plastics”.That commitment is made in light of the Assembly Commission’s approach to sustainability of seeking to reduce the overall waste generated across all recycling streams rather than providing an alternative to fill the gap. However, it is worth noting the plastic bottles that are offered for sale within the catering outlets are all fully recyclable while cans are provided for some product lines.Single use plastic containers and cutlery are being replaced with more sustainable and compostable alternatives.  All pass holders in Parliament Buildings are actively encouraged to bring their own reusable bottles and cups to the restaurant or for takeaway use.  Ceramic mugs, glasses and stainless-steel cutlery is available for use and jugs of water are provided at all meetings and events rather than bottles of water.Further to this, the Assembly Commission uses a Department of Finance collaborative agreement to ensure that zero waste is sent to landfill from Parliament Buildings. The Assembly Commission will continue to consider opportunities to improve its environmental performance in Parliament Buildings and specifically within the catering outlets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support he is providing to takeaway businesses in Northern Ireland, in light of reports that half of Northern Ireland’s takeaways are at threat of closure due to rising costs.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure that businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including input cost pressures for small businesses. As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition, the announcement by the Prime Minister of an Energy Price Guarantee and her continued commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.  My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.The outlook remains challenging but I am hopeful for the future. I am investing in those areas where we can gain the best long-term outcomes, which will help us build greater economic resilience for the future.  For example, I have published an energy strategy which will deliver a step change in how we move away from our reliance on overseas, imported fossil fuels. Alongside our investment in skills this will support the delivery of our ambitious 10X Economic Vision for the benefit of all our people.In practical terms, Invest Northern Ireland’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Support for businesses provides a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable local businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of rising energy prices, what additional support is available to people who require medical machines such as oxygen machines in their homes.
answer_text: All patients prescribed home oxygen concentrators have the associated electricity costs reimbursed fully.  After the first six months, reimbursements are made using a series of estimated and actual readings.This system “self-calibrates” over time. However if patients are still concerned in the interim, they can telephone their actual readings to BOC (the supplier of the home oxygen service) on a monthly basis and this will ensure even greater accuracy.In early 2022, due to rising energy costs, correspondence was sent to every patient who uses home oxygen to assure them that they would be reimbursed fully for any electricity costs associated with their concentrators and that if they were concerned they could contact BOC with readings and get a refund on a monthly basis.  Electricity reimbursement rates are continually reviewed and increased in line with external price changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is considering introducing a statutory right to time off work for people undergoing fertility treatment.
answer_text: Good employers recognise the potential impact undergoing fertility treatment can have on the ability of employees to work during this period. They have flexible supportive working policies in place such as homeworking, flexible hours or compassionate leave. I would encourage all employers to offer such flexible and supportive working arrangements for their staff.There are no plans at present to introduce a statutory tight to time off for persons undergoing fertility treatment.  An immediate focus and priority for my Department is the progression of paid miscarriage leave and domestic abuse safe leave.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1159/22-27, (i) which recommendations from the report will he be addressing, and (ii) for a timescale for the competition of the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: (i) The Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) included the assessment of a number of different options to enhance placemaking and active travel provision. The report recommends that  3 options be taken forward for further consideration. This work is ongoing.  (ii) A timescale for the future procurement and construction of the project cannot be confirmed at this time as it is dependent upon a number of factors, including the completion of any follow up work resulting from the PATR and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to introduce a fund similar to the Innovative Medicines Fund, recently announced by the Department of Health and Social Care.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange Scheme.
answer_text: The Place making and Active Travel Review, which aimed to maximise the social, economic and environmental benefits of the York Street Interchange project and its contribution to the future development of Belfast, has now been completed. The report identified a number of options to enhance place making and improve active travel provision. Work is ongoing to refine the shortlisted options and consider factors relating to their potential implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to the UK Government’s ban on charging ground rent on leases in England and Wales, what steps his Department is taking to reform ground rent.
answer_text: The system of land tenure in England and Wales differs substantially from land law in this jurisdiction. In England and Wales, issues around exponential increases in ground rents have necessitated reform. This issue has not been raised by many existing tenants in this jurisdiction and following the introduction of the Property (NI) Order 1997, no new ground rents, or increases to ground rents, have been permitted in dwelling houses. The reform of the law on ground rent is therefore not an area currently under consideration by the Department for the purposes of legislation.It is possible for a tenant to buy out their ground rent in this jurisdiction. Information on how to do this is available on the NI Direct website.
